figures.caption: Fig. 2. An illustration of the data curation pipeline. Egocentric videos are first segmented into 10-second audio clips. These clips are then captioned using Qwen2.5 Omni [9]. In Stage 1 (Sound-Source Match), an LLM (LLaMA3-70B [10]) associates these audio captions with narrations from the video to match sounds with their sources, distinguishing between foreground and background noises. Finally, in Stage 2 (Q/A Generation), the same LLM generates question-answer pairs based on the matched sound sources, including pairs designed to test for ”hallucinations” by asking about sounds that are not present.

Fig. 4. Agreement scores between automatic metrics and human evaluation (HE). We compare three automatic evaluation metrics: string matching (SM), embedding similarity (ES), and LLMas-judge (LAJ) on Qwen2.5 Omni [9] responses. Among these, LAJ achieves the highest agreement with human evaluation.
Fig. 5. Qualitative results. We show example results of the top two performing models Qwen2.5 Omni [9] and VideoLLaMA2 [8].

Fig. 1. Audio hallucinations in AV-LLMs. For example, VideoLLAMA2 [8] incorrectly infers the foreground sound “sound of digging” (shown in blue) from the presence of digging tools, in the video-only (no audio) inference path. Importantly, this visual bias persists even when audio is provided: in the video+audio path, the model continues to rely on the visual cue, hallucinating the foreground sound “digging” and additionally hallucinating the background sound “dog barking” (shown in red).
